Voice 1 (vast-llama3-8b first): Recommends centralized payment gateway configuration management system, using AWS Config or Google Cloud Asset Inventory to monitor/enforce compliance, using Stripe API webhook to apply settings when domain created/updated, using Stripe Payment Method Management, create reusable IaC template (Terraform?), use Stripe docs. Confidence 0.9. Risks: complexity, testing, conflicts.

Voice 2 (stepfun-flash): Suggests using Stripe's prebuilt Checkout with a shared configuration template hosted on central CDN, versioned GitHub repo, enforce consistent fields: mandatory logo upload to shared S3 bucket, mandatory tax ID collection for B2B, mandatory 3D Secure 2 for >$10, use Stripe Customer Portal with shared template, standardize usage-based billing line items with shared Price ID catalog, enforce same success/cancel URL patterns, shared post-purchase webhook with same email template, central analytics dashboard in Stripe Sigma. Deploy across domains using Stripe's Configuration as Code (CaC) with Stripe CLI to push same Checkout template to all linked Stripe accounts. Numbers: cuts per-domain setup from 8-12 hours to <30 minutes, reduces payment failure rates by 15-20% (per Stripe 2024 SaaS benchmark), PCI DSS SAQ A compliance automatically. Risks: custom checkout needs, alternative payment methods, regional regulations.

Voice 3 (stepfun-3.6): Proposes technical architecture: central Stripe configuration service (Node.js/Express API) to create Checkout Sessions, single Stripe account or multiple connected accounts, standardize product/price IDs, deploy shared JavaScript library that initializes Stripe.js with same publishable key and redirects to checkout session, same success/cancel URL pattern, shared webhook endpoints, common CSS for Elements, environment variables. Mentions Stripe Checkout supports 135+ currencies and 35+ payment methods. Possibly conversion increase? Not fully detailed. Also mentions using Stripe's "Checkout Session" API. Confidence? Not given but likely high. Risks? Not listed but implied.

Voice 4 (ollama-gemma3 first): Phased approach: Phase 1 (6-12 months): Stripe API standardization, domain-specific configuration stored in centralized DB (AWS DynamoDB), security hardening (MFA, fraud detection, PCI DSS), microservices architecture, CI/CD. Phase 2 (12-24 months): automated flow adaptation, personalization engine, integration with existing .ai tools, user feedback loop. Metrics: checkout completion rate, AOV, support ticket volume. Risks: Stripe API changes, domain-specific complexity, data privacy compliance, security vulnerabilities. Resources: Stripe docs, security guide, AWS DynamoDB.

Voice 5 (ollama-gemma3 second): Also phased: Phase 1: Centralized data model, automated flow mapping using Stripe's 'Flow Mapping' feature (maybe not real?), machine learning to identify friction, adopt Stripe's 'Adaptive Flow' technology, initial focus on basic payment methods. Phase 2: Customizable UI elements, address standardization, localized content/currency, fraud prevention/compliance. Phase 3: Real-time analytics dashboard, automated error reporting, feedback loop. Resources: Stripe docs, Adaptive Flow (URL: https://stripe.com/adaptive-flow), developer docs. Risks: complexity, API changes.

Voice 6 (vast-llama3-8b second): Centralized configuration management using Terraform, base template in GitHub, separate module per domain importing base template and overriding settings, automated testing with Jest/Pytest. Confidence 0.9. Risks: complexity, version control, resources.

Voice 7 (vast-llama3-8b third): Centralized configuration management using Ansible or Terraform, template engine to generate configs, monitoring system for payment metrics, feature flagging system (LaunchDarkly). Confidence 0.9. Risks: complexity, security risks, need for detailed analysis.

We see some common themes: centralized configuration management, using IaC (Terraform, Ansible), using Stripe's prebuilt Checkout or custom integration, shared components, monitoring, CI/CD, security/compliance.
